title: Letters from a Stoic
subtitle: Epistulae Morales ad Lucilium (Moral Letters to Lucilius)
author: Seneca (the Younger)

recommendedTranslation.title: Letters from a Stoic
recommendedTranslation.note: Selected and translated with an introduction by Robin Campbell. The most widely available English edition, containing 42 of the 124 surviving letters with helpful notes and index.

alternativeTranslations.publisher: Loeb Classical Library / Harvard University Press
alternativeTranslations.note: Complete three-volume Loeb edition with facing Latin text. The definitive scholarly edition for serious students.
Complete translation of all 124 letters. Authoritative modern scholarship with extensive notes.
Selected letters in Oxford World's Classics series. Solid translation with good introductions.

themes: Philosophy as Medicine for the Soul
excerpt: A collection of 124 philosophical letters written by Roman statesman Seneca the Younger in the final years of his life (63–65 AD), addressed to his friend Lucilius Junior, procurator of Sicily. Far more polished and systematic than the private journal of Marcus Aurelius, these letters use everyday observations as springboards into Stoic ethical instruction: how to face death without fear, why virtue is the only true good, the proper use of wealth, the value of friendship, and the art of tranquility. Seneca's distinctive contribution to Stoicism was transforming its austere doctrines into an accessible, humane, and deeply personal philosophy — one that directly shaped Christian ethics, Montaigne's essays, and the modern Stoic revival.
